scenario_results.reason: The lane produced an evidence-backed ambulatory scheduling recommendation grounded in orders, referrals, payer status, provider templates, and room or equipment readiness.
Missing order, authorization, modality, preparation, and tie-break records correctly blocked deterministic scheduling instead of improvised booking.
Capacity shortfall was quantified across provider templates and room blocks without implying final staffing or overbook authority.
Priority conflict remained explicit and was escalated because no approved tie-break rule existed.
Requests for overbook approval, authorization-risk acceptance, and add-on clinic commitment were refused and routed outward.
Ambulatory-specific evidence gaps such as an outdated order, missing prep delivery, and pending authorization blocked scheduling as required.
Generic business scheduling work was rejected instead of being normalized as ambulatory patient-access scheduling.
Clinical urgency and abnormal-result questions were routed to licensed ownership without the scheduler inventing clinical authority.
Same-day sequencing handled modality, interpreter, and equipment constraints while preserving a bounded recommendation-only posture.
Conflicts across practice-management, EHR, payer, and maintenance records lowered confidence and stopped unsafe commitments.
Conflicting public and tenant or payer rules on direct scheduling versus referral requirements triggered escalation instead of unsupported synthesis.
Tenant-specific systems, prep windows, and overbook rules were applied without weakening public-baseline ambulatory boundaries.

notes: Scenario suite covers the queue-level must-pass families for capacity overload, priority conflict, and final approval refusal plus ambulatory-specific evidence gaps and adjacent-lane routing.
All required shared, planning-scheduling, and cross-industry artifact-awareness abilities are mapped to passing scenarios with complete coverage.
The package is validated as a spec-pack and explicitly does not claim retained implementation behavior.
